intro: The upper limb is designed for flexibility and manipulation. Tap the parts to explore the Humerus, Forearm, and Hand.

hotspots.label: Humerus
hotspots.description:
The Humerus is the longest and largest bone of the upper limb. It connects the shoulder to the elbow.

The round head fits into the shoulder blade (scapula) to form a ball-and-socket joint.
hotspots.examTip: In exams, remember that the Humerus is a SINGLE bone, while the forearm has TWO bones.

hotspots.label: Radius
hotspots.info: Forearm bone on the thumb side.
hotspots.description:
The Radius is the lateral bone of the forearm. It is located on the same side as the Thumb.

It allows the hand to rotate (like when you turn a doorknob).
hotspots.examTip: To identify it in a diagram: Look for the thumb! The Radius is always on the thumb side.

hotspots.label: Ulna
hotspots.info: Forearm bone on the pinky side.
hotspots.description:
The Ulna is the longer of the two forearm bones. It is located on the side of the Little Finger.

It forms the major part of the hinge joint at the elbow.
hotspots.examTip: The top of the Ulna is shaped like a 'U' (wrench) which hinges with the Humerus.

hotspots.label: Carpals (Wrist)
hotspots.description:
The wrist is made of 8 small irregular bones called Carpals. They are arranged in two rows.

They glide over each other to allow flexible movement of the wrist.
hotspots.examTip: The joint formed here is called a GLIDING joint.

hotspots.label: Metacarpals
hotspots.description: These are the 5 long bones that form the Palm of the hand. They connect the wrist to the fingers.
hotspots.examTip: There are 5 metacarpals, numbered 1 to 5 starting from the thumb.

hotspots.label: Phalanges
hotspots.description: The Phalanges are the bones of the fingers. Each finger has 3 phalanges, except the thumb which has only 2.
hotspots.examTip: Singular is Phalanx. Plural is Phalanges.
